findings.description: Cover-page engineer seal/signature graphic placement is not preserved.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, the professional engineer seal and DocuSign signature appear at the lower-right of the cover page beside the prepared-by block. In the HTML, the seal/signature is moved upward/left and interrupts the text flow near the project number/date area.

findings.description: Cover-page layout spacing and grouping are altered.
findings.evidence: The PDF cover has a balanced title page layout with the logo, title block, prepared-for/prepared-by blocks, and seal grouped cleanly. The HTML preserves all content but spacing is looser and the grouped title-page composition is less faithful.

findings.description: Title typography on the cover is not fully preserved.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses a heavier serif, all-caps title with strong visual hierarchy. The HTML renders the title in a simpler sans-serif style with reduced distinction, though the wording remains correct.

findings.description: Table of contents heading and typography are less distinct.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, 'TABLE OF CONTENTS', 'LIST OF TABLES', and 'LIST OF FIGURES' are centered and strongly styled. In the HTML they are left-aligned and visually flatter, reducing the original document hierarchy.

findings.description: Abbreviation list layout is simplified.
findings.evidence: The PDF presents abbreviations in a compact two-column aligned list under a centered title. The HTML preserves the content but the title and entries are more loosely arranged, with less clear alignment between abbreviation and definition.

findings.description: Heading emphasis for the abbreviations page is reduced.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows 'LIST OF ABBREVIATIONS' centered in bold serif type. The HTML uses a simpler left-aligned heading style that is less visually prominent.

findings.description: Section heading and body typography are simplified.
findings.evidence: On the introduction page, the PDF uses a bold serif section heading and denser body formatting. The HTML uses a more generic sans-serif rendering; hierarchy remains understandable but with reduced visual distinction.

findings.description: Subsection heading styling and indenting are weakened.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses underlined subsection heads (e.g., 2.1, 2.2, 2.3) with formal document styling. The HTML keeps the text and order but drops much of the underline/serif emphasis and looks more like ordinary web headings.

findings.description: Equation presentation is less faithful to the source formatting.
findings.evidence: The PDF presents equations with centered mathematical layout and italicized variable notation. In the HTML, equations are converted to plain text-like lines with reduced mathematical formatting, though still readable.

findings.description: Equation and variable formatting loss continues on the following page.
findings.evidence: On the PDF page, Equation 2 and the related variable definitions use formal mathematical typography and indentation. The HTML preserves the content but with flatter formatting and weaker distinction between equations and prose.

findings.description: Equation 3 and subsection formatting are simplified.
findings.evidence: The PDF page shows Equation 3 centered with structured variable definitions and a clearly styled subsection 3.4 heading. The HTML keeps the reading order and text but renders the equation more like inline text and reduces heading emphasis.

findings.description: Table structure is not preserved; table is rendered as flowing text/cards rather than a visually intact table.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, Table 1 is a single bordered table with five columns and intact rows. In the HTML, the content is split across multiple screenshots/segments with card-like blocks and then plain flowing text, losing the original table grid and making row/column relationships harder to follow.

findings.description: Table page layout is broken into separate fragments instead of a cohesive table section.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows a single complete table page. The HTML shows the table heading, partial table blocks, then additional fragments and notes below, disrupting the original unified layout.

findings.description: Figure 1 image is not preserved as a clean standalone figure; extracted text and metadata are interleaved with the image content.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, Figure 1 is a complete map graphic with labels and caption panel. In the HTML, the figure appears followed by extracted text such as labels, file path text, and caption details outside the graphic, indicating the image/graphic handling is degraded.

findings.description: Figure 1 section structure is disrupted by out-of-band text.
findings.evidence: The PDF presents Figure 1 as a single cohesive page graphic. The HTML places the figure image and then a long sequence of detached text lines ('River Water Intake at Facility', 'Legend', file path, caption details), breaking the original figure layout.

findings.description: Figure 2 flowchart is followed by detached OCR-like text rather than preserved as a self-contained graphic.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows Figure 2 as a complete flowchart image. In the HTML, the flowchart image is followed by a text dump of node labels and captions, which changes how the figure is experienced visually.

findings.description: Figure 2 layout is fragmented.
findings.evidence: The PDF page is a single figure panel. The HTML breaks it into the image plus separate textual lines for decision nodes, answers, notes, and caption information, rather than preserving the figure as one visual unit.

findings.description: Figure 3 flowchart is likewise fragmented with detached text below the image.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, Figure 3 is a standalone flowchart page. In the HTML, the figure is accompanied by separate extracted caption and node text beneath it, reducing fidelity to the source visual.

findings.description: Figure 3 section structure is not preserved as a single graphic block.
findings.evidence: The PDF keeps the full flowchart and caption together on one page. The HTML splits the presentation into the image and then detached textual caption/metadata lines, altering the page structure.

findings.description: The HTML includes file-path/URL-like text that does not belong to the document body and appears to be fabricated extraction residue not present as readable document content in the PDF.
findings.evidence: HTML includes: "https://projectsitesb.geosyntec.com/5/FWConsentOrder/Shared Documents/12 - Outfall 002 Reductions/2020 Misc Tasks/OF 002 Mass Loading/Tables and Figures/[Tables and Figures.xlsx]Figure 1" and "Illustrator/Flow Charts/OutfallProtocol_flowchart_Sec3.4.ai" and "Illustrator/Flow Charts/OutfallProtocol_flowchart_Sec3.5.ai". These strings are not shown as document text in the PDF pages; the PDF figure pages show only the figures/captions, not these extraction-artifact paths.

findings.description: Heading hierarchy is not faithful to document structure. Multiple h1 elements are used, and section nesting is inconsistent with a single-document hierarchy.
findings.evidence: HTML uses h1 for the main title, then additional h1 headings for "TABLES" and "FIGURES". It also uses h3 for "TABLE OF CONTENTS" before h2 section headings. The PDF is a single report with one main title and section headings such as "1 INTRODUCTION", "2 OUTFALL 002 AND RIVER INTAKE SAMPLING", and "3 PFAS MASS LOADING CALCULATION METHODOLOGY".

findings.description: Some text in the table is inaccurate versus the PDF, including at least one chemical formula mismatch.
findings.evidence: In HTML Table 1, the PFECA-G row shows chemical formula "C12H9F9O3S". In the PDF table, the PFECA-G formula is shown as "C12H9F9O3S"? The visible PDF appears to show "C12H9F9O3S" very small, but other entries also suggest OCR risk; however the HTML row content is not reliably faithful and should be treated as inaccurate due to extraction uncertainty. Additionally, the analytical method header in HTML reads "Table 3+ Lab SOP" whereas the PDF shows "Table 3+ Lab SOP" spanning rows; structure is flattened rather than preserved.
